_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan (English I) style reading, batch F1. ~320-400 word abstract argumentative passages, 4 MCQs each focusing on attitude/inference/main idea/argument/vocabulary. Difficulty above CET-6. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: The division of labour, Adam Smith observed more than two centuries ago, is the engine of economic productivity. By breaking complex processes into narrow tasks at which workers become expert, societies have achieved prodigious gains in efficiency and wealth. Yet Smith himself, often remembered only as an apostle of the market, was alive to the human costs of this arrangement, costs that his more enthusiastic followers have tended to ignore. The worker confined to a few repetitive operations, Smith warned, may grow 'as stupid and ignorant as it is possible for a human creature to become', his mind dulled by the very specialisation that makes him economically valuable. The efficiency of the system, in other words, may be purchased at the price of the development of the people within it. This tension has only sharpened in the modern economy. Specialisation has advanced to extraordinary degrees, and with it has come not only material abundance but a peculiar narrowing. The specialist, master of a tiny domain, may understand the larger system to which he contributes hardly at all, and may find little meaning in work whose purpose he cannot see. The standard reply is that the wealth generated by specialisation more than compensates for its discontents, funding the leisure and education in which a fuller humanity may be pursued outside of work. There is force in this. But it concedes the essential point: that for many, work itself has ceased to be a site of human development, becoming merely a means to an income, to be endured rather than embraced. Whether a society can be called truly prosperous when so much of its labour is experienced as a diminishment of those who perform it is a question that economics, preoccupied with measurable output, is ill-equipped to answer, but which it cannot forever avoid. That the discipline so rarely raises it is not a sign that the question does not matter, but a measure of how narrowly we have come to define prosperity itself — as a sum to be counted rather than a condition to be lived, and as a property of nations rather than of the persons who compose them.

questions.prompt: What did Smith recognise that his more enthusiastic followers have ignored?
options: The human costs of the division of labour.

questions.prompt: Efficiency, the writer suggests, may be purchased at the price of what?
options: The development of the people within the system.

questions.prompt: What is the 'standard reply' to the discontents of specialisation?
options: That specialisation is harmful and should stop.
That the wealth it generates compensates, funding leisure and education outside work.

questions.prompt: What question does the writer say economics is ill-equipped to answer?
options: Whether a society is truly prosperous when its labour diminishes those who perform it.

sets.explain_zh: 细节/态度(B 斯密看到分工的人性代价、被追随者忽视)、细节(B 以系统中人的发展为代价换效率)、细节(B 标准回应:所创财富可补偿、资助工作之外的发展)、主旨/推断(B 当劳动贬损劳动者时社会是否真繁荣——经济学难答)。

sets.passage: We are naturally inclined to read history backwards, treating what happened as what was bound to happen. The present, with its apparent solidity, seems the inevitable destination of the past, and we construct narratives in which each event leads, as if by necessity, to the next. This habit of mind, comforting though it is, profoundly distorts our understanding. For history, examined closely, is shot through with contingency — with moments at which events might easily have unfolded otherwise. A battle turned by a sudden change of weather, a discovery made by accident, a leader felled by an untimely illness: again and again, the great currents of history have been diverted by small and unpredictable causes. The world we inhabit is but one of countless worlds that might have been. This is not to deny that broad forces — economic, geographic, technological — shape the range of the possible. But within the space these forces define, chance and individual choice retain a power that the deterministic histories, with their air of inevitability, systematically conceal. Why does this matter? Partly because the illusion of inevitability breeds a false fatalism, the sense that things could not have been otherwise and, by extension, cannot now be changed. If the present was the only possible outcome of the past, then the future, too, may seem fixed, beyond the reach of human agency. Recognising the contingency of history restores a truer and more bracing picture: that the world is not the unfolding of a predetermined design, but the precarious, ever-uncertain product of forces and choices that might, at any moment, have tended otherwise — and that the future, likewise, remains genuinely open, ours to shape. To grasp this is to recover a sense of responsibility that determinism quietly dissolves: if nothing was ever simply bound to happen, then what happens next is not fated either, but will be decided, in some real part, by what we choose to do with a freedom we are too often persuaded we do not possess.

questions.prompt: What habit of mind does the writer criticise?
options: The habit of studying history at all.
Treating what happened as what was bound to happen.

questions.prompt: What does 'contingency' refer to in the passage?
options: Moments at which events might easily have unfolded otherwise.

questions.prompt: What does the illusion of inevitability breed?
options: A false fatalism, the sense that nothing can now be changed.

questions.prompt: What truer picture does recognising contingency restore?
options: That the world is contingent and the future remains genuinely open to shape.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 把'发生的'当成'注定的')、词义(B contingency=本可不同的偶然性)、细节(B 滋生'无可改变'的虚假宿命感)、主旨/推断(B 世界是偶然之产物、未来真正开放可塑)。

sets.passage: The notion that privacy is something we might reasonably wish to protect rests on assumptions so familiar that we rarely examine them. Yet the technologies of the present have rendered those assumptions newly urgent, for they have made possible a degree of surveillance that earlier ages could scarcely have imagined, and in doing so have forced a question we have long been able to avoid: what, exactly, is privacy for? A common but inadequate answer is that privacy serves to conceal wrongdoing — that those with nothing to hide have nothing to fear. This formulation, superficially reasonable, collapses under scrutiny. Privacy is not the preserve of the guilty; it is a condition of ordinary human dignity. We draw the curtains not because our domestic lives are criminal, but because the capacity to withdraw from observation, to possess a sphere that is ours alone, is bound up with our sense of ourselves as autonomous persons. More troubling still is the subtle effect that the awareness of being watched exerts upon behaviour. Under observation, real or suspected, people grow cautious, conforming, less willing to dissent or to experiment. A society under comprehensive surveillance may thus become not merely less free but less alive, its citizens shrinking into a wary uniformity. The chilling of expression need not be deliberately imposed; it follows, almost mechanically, from the mere knowledge that one is seen. The defenders of expanding surveillance speak the reassuring language of security, and the dangers they cite are often real. But the trade they propose is rarely stated honestly. What is surrendered is not merely a quantum of personal secrecy, but something closer to the conditions of a free and self-possessed existence — surrendered, moreover, in exchange for a security that, history suggests, the powerful are seldom slow to turn to their own ends. The bargain is therefore never quite the simple exchange of a little privacy for a little safety that it is made to appear; it is, more truly, a slow ceding of the very conditions under which a free people remains free, and once those conditions are surrendered they are seldom recovered intact.

questions.prompt: How does the writer regard the claim 'nothing to hide, nothing to fear'?
options: As inadequate; it collapses under scrutiny.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say privacy actually is?
options: A condition of ordinary human dignity, bound up with autonomy.

questions.prompt: What effect does awareness of being watched exert on behaviour?
options: People grow cautious and conforming, less willing to dissent.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say is really surrendered to expanding surveillance?
options: Something close to the conditions of a free, self-possessed existence.

sets.explain_zh: 细节/态度(B 该说法经不起推敲、不充分)、细节(B 隐私是人之尊严与自主的条件)、细节(B 被注视使人谨慎从众、不敢异见)、主旨/推断(B 交出的近乎自由自主之生存的条件)。

sets.passage: What makes a work of art valuable? The question seems straightforward, yet attempts to answer it have divided philosophers for centuries, and the difficulty is more than academic. For on it depends whether we regard our judgements of art as expressions of genuine truth or as mere matters of personal taste. One ancient view holds that artistic value is objective — that a great painting or symphony possesses excellence in itself, independent of any observer, much as a mathematical proof is valid whether or not anyone appreciates it. On this account, those who fail to perceive a masterpiece's greatness are simply mistaken, their faculties undeveloped or their attention insufficient. The opposing view insists that value lies not in the object but in the response it evokes — that beauty is, in the worn phrase, in the eye of the beholder. There is no fact of the matter about a work's worth; there are only the varied reactions of individuals, none more correct than another. To call a work great is merely to report that one happens to like it. Each position, pressed to its conclusion, yields absurdities. Pure objectivism cannot explain why informed and sensitive judges disagree, or why artistic taste varies so profoundly across cultures and ages. Pure subjectivism, meanwhile, cannot account for the stubborn sense that some judgements really are better than others — that the person who ranks a greeting-card verse above the finest poetry has not merely a different taste but a worse one. The truth, if there is one, seems to lie in some uncomfortable middle region, where judgements of art are neither wholly objective nor wholly arbitrary, but disciplined responses, educable and arguable, that aspire to a correctness they can never quite secure. To say this is not to abandon judgement but to understand its peculiar character: that in matters of art, as perhaps in much of life, we reason towards a truth we cannot finally prove, guided by a cultivated sensibility that is neither mere fact nor mere whim, but something harder to name and more valuable than either.

questions.prompt: What does the objective view of artistic value hold?
options: That beauty is merely personal taste.
That a work possesses excellence in itself, independent of any observer.

questions.prompt: What does the subjective view insist?
options: That value lies in the response a work evokes, not in the object.
That all works are equally great.
That taste is fixed at birth.

questions.prompt: What can pure subjectivism not account for?
options: The stubborn sense that some judgements really are better than others.

questions.prompt: Where does the truth seem to lie, according to the writer?
options: In a middle region, neither wholly objective nor wholly arbitrary.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 客观论:作品自身具卓越、与观者无关)、细节(B 主观论:价值在于所引发的反应)、细节(B 主观论无法解释'有些判断确实更好')、主旨/推断(B 真理在不全客观也不全任意的中间地带)。

sets.passage: Few ideas are as deeply woven into the modern outlook as that of progress — the conviction that history moves, however unevenly, towards improvement, and that the present is, on balance, better than the past and worse than the future will be. So natural does this assumption feel that we seldom notice it is an assumption at all, still less a relatively recent one, foreign to most of human history. The case for progress is not without substance. By many measurable standards — life expectancy, material comfort, the reach of education, the reduction of certain cruelties — the trajectory of recent centuries has indeed bent upward, and to deny this would be a perverse ingratitude towards those whose labours secured it. Yet the idea of progress, uncritically held, carries hidden dangers. It can breed a complacent condescension towards the past, which we judge by our own standards and find wanting, blind to the wisdom it may have possessed and we have lost. It can license a reckless contempt for the present in the name of an imagined future, justifying real sacrifices today for benefits that may never come. And it can lull us into a passive faith that improvement is somehow guaranteed, the automatic product of time, rather than the precarious achievement of effort that may at any moment be reversed. The truth is that progress, where it occurs, is neither inevitable nor irreversible nor uniform. Gains in one domain may be purchased by losses in another; advances may be undone; and the very notion of 'improvement' smuggles in a judgement of values that is itself contestable. To believe in progress is permissible; to believe in it as a law of history, indifferent to our choices, is to surrender precisely the agency on which any genuine progress depends. The belief in progress, rightly held, is thus a spur to effort rather than a substitute for it; held wrongly, as a guarantee written into the very nature of things, it becomes the most insidious enemy of the improvements it promises, lulling into passivity those whose vigilance alone could secure them.

questions.prompt: What does the writer note about the idea of progress?
options: That it is ancient and universal.
That it is an assumption, and a relatively recent one.
That it is a law of nature.

questions.prompt: What is one hidden danger of the idea of progress?
options: A complacent condescension towards the past and a passive faith that improvement is guaranteed.

questions.prompt: Does the writer deny that progress has occurred?
options: Yes, the writer denies it entirely.
No; the writer acknowledges measurable gains.
The writer says progress is impossible.
The writer claims the past was always better.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's central claim about progress?
options: That it is a law of history indifferent to our choices.
That it is neither inevitable nor irreversible and depends on our choices.
That the future is wholly fixed.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 进步是假设、且较晚近)、细节(B 对过去的傲慢与对进步'必然'的被动信仰)、细节/态度(B 不否认确有可量化的进步)、主旨(B 进步非必然不可逆、取决于我们的选择)。

sets.passage: Modern societies face a dilemma that grows sharper with each passing decade. The problems of governance — climate, finance, public health, technology — have become so complex and technical that their competent management seems to require expertise possessed by only a few. Yet the legitimacy of democratic government rests on the principle that the people, not a specialist elite, should rule. How are these to be reconciled? The temptation, felt by many thoughtful people, is to lean towards expertise — to let those who understand a problem decide it, and to regard the uninformed opinions of the public as, at best, an obstacle. There is an obvious appeal here: we would not put medical decisions to a popular vote, so why entrust the management of an economy to the untutored? But the analogy, on examination, misleads. The questions that confront a society are rarely purely technical. They involve, beneath their technical surface, judgements of value — about how risks and benefits should be distributed, about what we owe to future generations, about the kind of society we wish to inhabit. On such questions the expert holds no special authority; his expertise concerns means, not ends, and to disguise a value-laden choice as a merely technical one is a characteristic and dangerous deception. The reconciliation, if there is one, must therefore lie not in choosing between expertise and democracy but in properly relating them: in a division of labour whereby experts illuminate the likely consequences of alternatives, while the choice among those alternatives — being a choice of values — remains with the citizenry. A democracy that ignores its experts is foolish; but one that surrenders to them its fundamental choices has ceased, in the ways that matter most, to be a democracy at all. The task before us, then, is not to choose between the rule of the wise and the rule of the many, but to design institutions in which each does the work it is fitted for — a far harder and less glamorous undertaking than either the technocrat's dream or the populist's, but the only one that does justice to both knowledge and consent.

questions.prompt: What dilemma do modern societies face?
options: Complex problems seem to require expertise, yet democracy requires that the people rule.

questions.prompt: Why does the medical analogy mislead?
options: It does not mislead at all.
Because societal questions involve judgements of value, not purely technical ones.
Because doctors are not really experts.

questions.prompt: What does the writer call a 'dangerous deception'?
options: Disguising a value-laden choice as a merely technical one.

questions.prompt: What reconciliation does the writer propose?
options: Experts illuminate consequences while citizens make the value choices.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 复杂问题需专长、而民主要人民统治)、细节(B 社会问题含价值判断、非纯技术)、细节(B 把价值选择伪装成纯技术问题是危险欺骗)、主旨(B 专家说明后果、公民做价值抉择)。

sets.passage: For the better part of a century, economic growth has been the unquestioned goal of public policy, the measure against which the success of governments is judged and the remedy prescribed for almost every social ill. Growth, we are assured, lifts the poor, funds public services, and quiets social discontent. To question it has seemed not merely mistaken but faintly irresponsible. Yet a growing chorus of economists and ecologists has begun to ask whether endless growth, on a planet of finite resources, is either possible or desirable. Their argument is not against prosperity but against a particular and increasingly questionable assumption: that ever-rising production and consumption are the same as, or reliably produce, human well-being. The evidence for this assumption is weaker than its dominance would suggest. Beyond the levels needed to meet basic needs, the relationship between a society's wealth and the satisfaction of its members grows tenuous; richer nations are not reliably happier ones. Meanwhile, the environmental costs of relentless growth — the exhaustion of resources, the destabilising of the climate — mount towards a reckoning that no amount of future production can repay. The difficulty is that our entire economic order is built upon the expectation of growth, and depends upon it for its stability; a stationary economy, under present arrangements, means not contentment but crisis — unemployment, debt, decline. To imagine an economy that could flourish without growing is thus to imagine a transformation more profound than any mere adjustment of policy. Whether such a transformation is possible is perhaps the central economic question of the century — and the fact that it is so rarely asked is itself a measure of how completely the assumption of growth has colonised our imagination. That we can scarcely imagine such an economy is not evidence that none could exist, but only of how thoroughly a single idea has come to bound our sense of the possible — and the first step towards any alternative is simply to recover the freedom to ask whether the assumption was ever true.

questions.prompt: What assumption do the critics question?
options: That ever-rising production and consumption are the same as human well-being.

questions.prompt: What does the evidence about wealth and satisfaction show?
options: That beyond basic needs the link between wealth and satisfaction grows tenuous.
That there is no link whatsoever.

questions.prompt: Why is questioning growth so difficult?
options: Because the whole economic order is built on growth and depends on it for stability.

questions.prompt: What does the rarity of the question reveal, according to the writer?
options: How completely the assumption of growth has colonised our imagination.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 质疑'增产即福祉'的假设)、细节(B 超过基本需求后财富与满足的联系变弱)、细节(B 整个经济秩序依赖增长以维持稳定)、主旨/推断(B 此问罕被提出、显示增长假设已殖民我们的想象)。

sets.passage: We tend to think of memory as a kind of recording — a faithful, if sometimes incomplete, archive of our past, which we consult much as we might replay a film. This intuitive picture is almost entirely wrong, and its wrongness has consequences that reach from the courtroom to our most intimate sense of who we are. Memory, psychologists have established, is not a reproduction but a reconstruction. Each time we recall an event, we do not retrieve a stored copy but actively rebuild the memory from fragments, filling the gaps with inference, expectation and suggestion. And — this is the unsettling part — the reconstructed memory is then re-stored, altered, so that the act of remembering itself changes what is remembered. Our most vivid recollections may be, in significant part, our own later inventions. The implications are profound. Eyewitness testimony, long treated as the gold standard of evidence, has been shown to be alarmingly unreliable; confident, detailed memories of events have been demonstrated, in the laboratory, to be wholly false, implanted by nothing more than suggestion. The very confidence with which a memory is held bears little relation to its accuracy. Beyond the practical consequences lies a deeper unease. If our memories are not the faithful record we imagine, then the personal history on which we found our sense of identity is itself, in part, a fiction — a story we tell ourselves, continually revised, rather than a fixed account of what occurred. We are not, it seems, the reliable narrators of our own lives that we suppose ourselves to be, but the authors, half-knowing, of a past that never quite was. This is unsettling, but it need not be merely dispiriting; for if we are in part the authors of our own pasts, then we are not wholly the prisoners of them either, and the same reconstructive faculty that quietly distorts our memories also allows us, within limits, to reinterpret them and so to grow.

questions.prompt: What is memory, according to psychologists?
options: A faithful recording of the past.

questions.prompt: What is the 'unsettling part' the writer identifies?
options: The act of remembering itself changes what is remembered.

questions.prompt: What has been shown about eyewitness testimony?
options: That it is the reliable gold standard.
That it is alarmingly unreliable, and confidence bears little relation to accuracy.

questions.prompt: What deeper unease does this raise?
options: That the personal history founding our identity is, in part, a fiction.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 记忆是重建而非复制)、细节(B 回忆这一行为本身改变了所记之事)、细节(B 目击证词不可靠、信心与准确度关系甚微)、主旨/推断(B 支撑身份的个人史部分是虚构)。

sets.passage: Every technology, in extending our powers, also reshapes us in ways we seldom choose and rarely notice. The tool we take up to serve our purposes comes, in subtle ways, to alter the purposes themselves, and the user is transformed even as he uses. This double character of technology — at once liberating and shaping — lies at the heart of a disquiet that has accompanied technical progress since its beginnings. Consider how the technologies of communication, which promise to connect us, may also impoverish the connection they mediate. A conversation conducted through screens, however convenient, lacks the fullness of presence — the glance, the pause, the unspoken understanding — through which human beings have always principally communed. We may find ourselves, paradoxically, more connected and more alone, our relationships multiplied in number but thinned in substance. The deeper worry is not that any particular technology harms us, but that we adopt our technologies without ever deciding to, swept along by a logic that seems to unfold of its own accord. Each innovation, once available, becomes first an option, then an expectation, then a necessity, until a way of life that no one chose has quietly become the only one on offer. The person who would opt out finds that the world has been rebuilt around the assumption of participation. This is not an argument against technology, which would be as futile as it is foolish. It is a plea for a posture too rarely adopted towards our tools: one of conscious and critical appraisal, in which we ask not merely whether a technology can be made, or whether it is convenient, but whether the form of life it brings with it is one we have reason to want. To use our tools wisely, we must first refuse to be used by them. Such a refusal is not Luddism but its very opposite: the precondition of using our instruments as instruments, rather than drifting, half-aware, into lives shaped entirely by tools we never paused to question, in the service of ends we never paused to choose.

questions.prompt: What 'double character' of technology does the writer describe?
options: That it is at once liberating and shaping of the user.

questions.prompt: How may communication technologies impoverish connection?
options: They lack the fullness of presence, so we may be more connected yet more alone.
They have no effect on connection.

questions.prompt: What is the 'deeper worry' the writer identifies?
options: That technology often fails to work.
That we adopt technologies without ever choosing to, until a way of life no one chose becomes the only one.

questions.prompt: What does the writer plead for?
options: The total rejection of all technology.
A conscious, critical appraisal of the forms of life our tools bring.
Adopting every new technology at once.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 技术既解放又塑造使用者)、细节(B 屏幕交流缺乏'在场'之丰盈、令人更连又更孤)、细节(B 我们在不知不觉中被卷入、采用从未选择的生活方式)、主旨/态度(B 呼吁对工具作自觉而批判的省察)。

sets.passage: What is education for? The question, seemingly simple, conceals a contest between two conceptions so different that they can scarcely both be satisfied, and the unease that pervades modern schooling stems in no small part from our refusal to choose between them. The first conception is instrumental. Education, on this view, exists to equip the young with the skills and knowledge they will need to be productive workers and to prosper in the economy. Its success is measured by employment and earnings, and its curriculum is shaped accordingly, with the practical and the marketable favoured over the merely interesting. This view has the great merit of obvious utility, and in an anxious and competitive age it has come increasingly to dominate. The second conception is older and harder to defend in the language of utility. Education, it holds, exists to form a person — to cultivate the capacities for thought, judgement and appreciation through which a human life is enriched and made fully human, quite apart from any economic use. On this view, to study history or poetry not because it pays but because it deepens and liberates the mind is not a luxury but the very point of the enterprise. The two conceptions are not wholly incompatible, and the wisest education has always sought to honour both. But where they conflict, as in an age of scarcity and anxiety they increasingly do, a choice cannot be evaded, and the choice we are making is plain. As education is recast ever more completely as an instrument of economic production, we should at least be clear about what is being sacrificed: not merely an ornament, but a conception of the human being as something more than an instrument — a conception without which the very prosperity we pursue may prove strangely hollow. A society that has forgotten how to defend the second conception in any language but that of utility has not thereby refuted it; it has merely lost the words for something it will continue, obscurely, to miss, and may one day have to learn, painfully, to value again.

questions.prompt: What is the 'instrumental' conception of education?
options: That it exists to form a whole person.
That it exists to equip the young with skills to be productive workers and prosper.
That it exists mainly to study poetry.
That it exists to waste time.

questions.prompt: What is the second, older conception of education?
options: To help students earn the most money.
To form a person, cultivating thought, judgement and appreciation.

questions.prompt: Which conception, the writer says, increasingly dominates?
What does the writer say is being sacrificed?
options: A conception of the human being as something more than an instrument.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 工具论:培养能赚钱、能在经济中立足的劳动者)、细节(B 第二种:成全人、培养思考判断与鉴赏)、细节/态度(B 工具论日益占主导)、主旨(B 被牺牲的是'人不只是工具'的观念)。
